params.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each appear. Full separation. Worked example designation visible.
Equation builder appears showing empty template: blank × blank.

beats.text: You've been matching expressions to equal groups. Now YOU get to build them, and you'll learn to go the other way too, figuring out what an expression looks like as a picture. Let me show you how building works.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each. Full separation. Worked example.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: First, I ask myself: how many groups? Each bag is one group.

params.description: Bags highlight one by one as Guide counts.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four bags. That's 4 groups.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each. All bags highlighted after sequential count.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: Now I ask: how many in each group? Let me look inside one bag.

params.description: First bag zooms into focus, apples inside highlight.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3. Three apples. Let me check another...

elements.description: First bag in focus with 3 apples highlighted.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

params.description: Second bag highlights.

beats.text: Also 3. They're equal groups. Same number in every bag.

elements.description: Second bag highlighted showing 3 apples.
Empty equation template: blank × blank.

beats.text: So I have 4 groups of 3. Now watch how I build the expression.

params.description: First slot glows with subtle label "groups".

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot glowing with label "groups". Second slot empty.

beats.text: The first number is how many groups. I click 4.

params.description: 4 appears in first slot.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot filled with 4. Second slot empty.

params.description: Second slot glows with label "in each".

beats.text: The second number is how many in each group. I click 3.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
First slot shows 4. Second slot glowing with label "in each".

params.description: 3 appears in second slot. Complete expression shows: 4 × 3.

beats.text: I built 4 times 3. This is called an expression. An expression uses numbers and symbols to show the math. No equal sign. Just the numbers and the times symbol.

elements.description: 4 bags with 3 apples each visible.
Complete expression: 4 × 3.

params.description: 3 bags appear on screen, each containing 6 items. Equal groups visual showing concrete context.
Equation builder appears below equal groups. Two blank slots with multiplication symbol between them.

beats.text: Your turn to build an expression. First question: how many groups do you see? Second question: how many in each group? Now build the expression.
Build the expression for the picture.

validator.description: Student selected 3 × 6, correct answer

params.description: 3 × 6 fills into equation builder slots to confirm selection.

beats.text: Right. You built 3 times 6. That expression shows 3 groups of 6.

validator.description: Student selected 6 × 3: reversed the numbers

params.description: Bags highlight one by one.

beats.text: Count the bags first. How many bags do you see? That number goes in the first slot.

validator.description: Student selected 3 + 6: used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: Equal groups structure highlights: bags and items within.

beats.text: These are equal groups. We use multiplication, not addition. Look for the times symbol between the numbers.

validator.description: Student selected 6 × 6: counted items instead of recognizing group structure

params.description: First bag highlights to show it is one group.

beats.text: Each bag is one group. Count how many bags there are. Then count how many items are in one bag.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially as Guide counts.

beats.text: Let me show you. I count the bags: 1, 2, 3. Three bags. That is 3 groups, so the first number is 3. Now I look inside one bag and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six items in each bag. That is 6 in each group, so the second number is 6. The expression is 3 times 6.

elements.description: 3 bags with 6 items each. Equal groups visual in reading mode.
Equation builder showing 3 × 6 expression filled in.

beats.text: Different containers, bags or boxes, but the SAME structure. Both are 2 groups of 5. Both build the same expression: 2 times 5.

beats.text: Equal groups work the same way no matter what holds them.

beats.text: All of these show bags with items inside. But can I write a multiplication expression for all of them? Remember, multiplication shows EQUAL groups.
Select the pictures where every bag has the SAME number inside.
Select ALL the pictures that show equal groups.

validator.description: Student selected A and C, both equal groups, correct

beats.text: Right. The first one has 3 bags with 4 in each, equal groups, 3 times 4. The third one has 4 bags with 3 in each, equal groups, 4 times 3. The other two? Different amounts in the bags so they have unequal groups. We can't write a multiplication expression for those.

validator.description: Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers but not all (either A or C, not both)

params.description: Option A highlights.
Option C highlights.

beats.text: You found one that works. Look at all the bags again. Is there another one where every bag has the same number?

validator.description: Branch 3: Student selected no correct answers (only B, only D, both B and D, or neither)

params.description: Option A highlights.

beats.text: Equal groups means every bag has the SAME amount. Look at the first picture. Count the items in each bag. Are they all the same?

validator.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect

params.description: Option B highlights.
Option D highlights.

beats.text: Some of your choices work, but check these highlighted ones. Count the items in each bag. Do they all have the same number?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A bags highlight sequentially showing 4, 4, 4.

beats.text: Let me show you. The first picture has 3 bags. Count the items: 4 in the first bag, 4 in the second, 4 in the third. All the same, so equal groups.

params.description: Option C bags highlight sequentially showing 3, 3, 3, 3.

beats.text: The third picture has 4 bags: 3 in each one. All the same, equal groups. The correct answers are A and C because every bag in those pictures has the same number inside.

params.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each appear on screen.
Equation builder appears with two blank slots and multiplication symbol. Tile palette shows tiles 1 through 10.

beats.text: You've learned that you need equal groups to write multiplication expressions and that equal groups work the same way no matter what holds them. These are important ideas for multiplication.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder with two blank slots and tile palette showing tiles 1 through 10.

beats.text: You've been choosing complete expressions to represent equal groups. Now you'll build them piece by piece using number tiles. Watch.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder with two blank slots and tile palette.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially.

beats.text: Notice all of the groups are equal. Same questions: How many groups?
5 boxes. That's 5 groups. I need the 5 tile in the first slot.

params.description: Guide demonstrates picking up 5 tile and placing it in first slot.
Tile 5 snaps into first slot. Expression now shows 5 × __.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder showing 5 × __ with second slot still blank.

params.description: Items in one box highlight.

beats.text: How many in each group?
2 items. I need the 2 tile in the second slot.

params.description: Guide demonstrates placing 2 tile in second slot.
Tile 2 snaps into second slot. Complete expression now shows 5 × 2.

beats.text: I built it piece by piece. 5 times 2. Now you try with tiles.

elements.description: 5 boxes with 2 items each.
Equation builder showing complete expression 5 × 2.

params.description: 2 bags with 4 items each appear on screen.
Equation builder appears below equal groups. Two blank slots with multiplication symbol between them. Tile palette shows numbers 1 through 10.

beats.text: Build the expression for these bags using tiles. First number: how many groups? Second number: how many in each group?
Build the expression.

validator.description: Student placed 2 × 4

beats.text: Right. 2 times 4 shows 2 groups of 4.

validator.description: Student reversed the numbers: placed 4 × 2 instead of 2 × 4

params.description: Bags highlight to draw attention to group count.

beats.text: You switched the numbers. Count the bags first. That's your groups number. Then count inside one bag. That's your in-each number.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt, not the reversed case

beats.text: Let's look closely. How many bags do you see?

validator.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt, not the reversed case

params.description: Bags highlight one by one as system counts.

beats.text: Count the bags: 1, 2. That's 2 groups. Now pick one bag and count what's inside. How many items?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight one by one. Then items in one bag highlight.
System places 2 tile in first slot, then 4 tile in second slot.
System places 4 tile in second slot.

beats.text: Here's how to build it. First I count the bags. 1, 2. Two bags means 2 groups, so I place 2 in the first slot. Then I look inside one bag and count the items. 1, 2, 3, 4. Four items means 4 in each group, so I place 4 in the second slot. The answer is 2 times 4, because that's what the picture shows: 2 groups of 4.

elements.description: 2 bags with 4 items each.
Equation builder showing 2 × 4. Both slots filled.

params.description: 3 boxes appear, each containing 5 items.
Equation builder appears with two blank slots and multiplication symbol. Tile palette shows 2, 3, 5, 8, 9, 12, 15.

beats.text: Build the expression. Same two questions: how many groups? How many in each?
Build the expression.

validator.description: Student placed 3 × 5

beats.text: That's it. 3 times 5.

validator.description: Student reversed the numbers, placing 5 × 3 instead of 3 × 5

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one to emphasize counting groups.

beats.text: You placed 5 times 3, but look at the groups. Count the boxes: 1, 2, 3. That's 3 groups. Now count inside one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. That's 5 in each group. What expression shows 3 groups of 5?

validator.description: Student placed an incorrect expression on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Count the boxes first. That's how many groups you have.

validator.description: Student placed an incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one as system counts.

beats.text: Let's think about this together. Count the boxes: 1, 2, 3. The first number is how many groups, so that's 3. Now look inside one box. Count those items. How many do you see in each?

validator.description: Student placed an incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially as system counts.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count the groups. That's the boxes: 1, 2, 3. Three groups. That number goes first, so I place the 3 tile.

params.description: System places 3 tile in first slot.

beats.text: Next, I count how many in each group. I look inside one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five items. That number goes second, so I place the 5 tile.

params.description: Items in one box highlight as system counts them.
System places 5 tile in second slot, completing 3 × 5.

beats.text: The expression is 3 times 5. That's what the picture shows: 3 groups of 5. The first number always tells you how many groups, and the second number tells you how many in each group.

elements.description: 3 boxes, each containing 5 items.
Equation builder showing 3 × 5. Tile palette: 2, 3, 5, 8, 9, 12, 15.

beats.text: The expression tells you what to look for. 2 times 6 MEANS 2 groups with 6 in each.

beats.text: The first number tells you how many groups. Look at the expression. How many groups do you need?

validator.description: Student selected option C: scattered items with no groups

params.description: The expression 5 × 3 highlights.

beats.text: Multiplication shows equal groups. Look for a picture with groups, not scattered items. How many groups do you see in the expression?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option A bags highlight one by one as system counts.

beats.text: Let me show you. The expression says 5 times 3. The first number, 5, tells us how many groups. Count the bags in Option A: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five groups. The second number, 3, tells us how many in each group. Look inside any bag: 3 items. Option A matches because it shows 5 groups with 3 in each. That is what 5 times 3 means.

Add boxes and items to show 4 × 5.

validator.description: Student set container count to 4, correct first step

params.description: Four empty containers appear in the workspace.

validator.description: Student set container count to incorrect value on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the first number in the expression. How many groups?

validator.description: Student set container count to incorrect value on second attempt

params.description: First number 4 highlights in the expression.

beats.text: The first number tells you how many groups. 4 times 5 means 4 groups. Set the container count to 4.

validator.description: Student set container count to incorrect value three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting container count to 4. Four empty containers appear.

beats.text: Let me show you. The first number in a multiplication expression is the number of groups. 4 times 5 means 4 groups. I set the container count to 4, and now we have 4 empty containers. That matches the first number in the expression.

elements.description: Equation builder showing 4 × 5 in expression mode.
Equal groups workspace with set_container_count tool active. Workspace empty, awaiting student input.

beats.text: Now add items to each box.

validator.description: Student set items per container to 5, completing 4 boxes of 5 items, correct

params.description: Five items populate each of the four containers.

beats.text: You built it. All of the groups are equal. 4 boxes with 5 items in each. 4 groups of 5.

validator.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value on first attempt

beats.text: Let's take another look. The second number tells you how many in each group.

validator.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value on second attempt

params.description: Second number 5 highlights in the expression.

beats.text: The second number tells you how many in each group. 4 times 5 means 5 in each. Set the items per container to 5.

validator.description: Student set items per container to incorrect value three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting items per container to 5. Five items populate each of the four containers.

beats.text: Here's how it works. The second number in a multiplication expression is how many in each group. 4 times 5 means 5 in each group. I set items per container to 5. Now each of the 4 containers has exactly 5 items. That's what 4 times 5 means: 4 groups with 5 in each.

elements.description: Equation builder showing 4 × 5 in expression mode.
Equal groups workspace showing 4 containers with 5 items each. Build complete.

params.description: Dots in one circle highlight.

beats.text: Now I count how many in each group. I look inside one circle: 1, 2, 3. Three dots, so the second number is 3. The expression is 2 times 3. That's what these circles show, 2 groups of 3.

beats.text: Right. 5 groups, 6 in each.

params.description: Numbers in expression highlight sequentially: first 5, then 6.
Option A demonstrates counting: circles pulse one by one showing 5 groups, then items in one circle highlight showing 6 in each.

beats.text: Here's how to read 5 times 6. The first number is groups. I count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five groups. The second number is in each group. I count inside one circle: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six in each. So 5 times 6 means 5 groups with 6 in each group. That's Option A.

Option B: 6 circles, 5 dots in each.
